questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
The General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) had operated since 1947 on the basis of periodic negotiating rounds among member countries. By the early 1990s, however, the expansion of global trade required a more permanent institutional framework. The Uruguay Round (1986-1994) produced a new agreement that transformed GATT into a permanent international organization with binding dispute settlement mechanisms and expanded coverage to include services and intellectual property.
— Adapted from a history of international trade institutions

questions.question: Which institution, established in 1995, replaced the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) and became the primary organization governing international trade rules?

choices.text: International Monetary Fund
choices.explanation: The IMF focuses on monetary cooperation and financial stability, not trade rules — it was established in 1944 at Bretton Woods.

choices.text: World Trade Organization
choices.explanation: The WTO replaced GATT in 1995 and became the main multilateral body for negotiating trade rules, resolving disputes, and promoting free trade.

choices.text: World Bank
choices.explanation: The World Bank provides development loans and technical assistance but does not govern international trade rules.

choices.text: United Nations Conference on Trade and Development
choices.explanation: UNCTAD is a UN body that discusses trade issues but lacks the binding dispute-resolution authority of the WTO.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the development of globalization after the Cold War

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
The new economic orthodoxy that emerged in the 1980s, associated with the governments of Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher, called for reducing the role of the state in the economy: cutting government spending and taxes, privatizing state-owned enterprises, deregulating financial markets, and opening economies to foreign trade and investment. This framework was then promoted globally by the International Monetary Fund and World Bank as a condition of their loans to developing nations.
— Adapted from a history of late 20th-century economic policy

questions.question: The term 'neoliberalism' in the context of late 20th-century globalization refers to which set of economic policies?

choices.text: State ownership of major industries and central economic planning
choices.explanation: State ownership and central planning describe socialist or command economies, which neoliberalism explicitly opposes.

choices.text: Protectionist tariffs and import-substitution industrialization
choices.explanation: Import-substitution industrialization was common in developing nations in the mid-20th century but is the opposite of neoliberal free-trade policies.

choices.text: Free markets, deregulation, privatization, and reduced government spending
choices.explanation: Neoliberalism advocates free market principles, including deregulation of industries, privatization of state enterprises, trade liberalization, and reduced social spending — associated with Thatcher and Reagan.

choices.text: Progressive taxation and strong welfare-state spending
choices.explanation: Progressive taxation and robust welfare states reflect Keynesian or social democratic policies, not neoliberalism.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which technological development most directly enabled the rapid expansion of global communication networks in the 1990s?

choices.text: The invention of the telegraph
choices.explanation: The telegraph was invented in the 1830s–1840s and transformed communication in the 19th century, not the 1990s.

choices.text: The development of satellite television
choices.explanation: Satellite television emerged in the 1960s–1970s and expanded global broadcasting but was not the key 1990s communication revolution.

choices.text: The World Wide Web and commercialized internet
choices.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee's World Wide Web (1991) and the commercialization of the internet in the 1990s fundamentally transformed global communication, commerce, and information exchange.

choices.text: The construction of transoceanic cable networks
choices.explanation: Transoceanic telegraph cables were laid in the 19th century; while fiber-optic cables expanded in the 1990s, the transformative development was the World Wide Web.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how technology has shaped globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), signed in 1994, eliminated most trade barriers between which three countries?

choices.text: United States, United Kingdom, and Canada
choices.explanation: The UK is not part of NAFTA; NAFTA was a North American agreement not including European nations.

choices.text: United States, Canada, and Mexico
choices.explanation: NAFTA linked the three North American economies in a free trade zone, eliminating most tariffs and creating an integrated regional market of 450 million people.

choices.text: United States, Mexico, and Brazil
choices.explanation: Brazil is a South American nation and was not part of NAFTA; Brazil belongs to MERCOSUR, a separate South American trade bloc.

choices.text: United States, Canada, and Japan
choices.explanation: Japan is not part of NAFTA; the agreement was specifically a North American regional trade arrangement.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how regional trade agreements have shaped global economic integration

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of a 'multinational corporation' in the context of globalization?

choices.text: A corporation owned jointly by multiple national governments
choices.explanation: Government-owned corporations are state enterprises, not multinationals; multinational corporations are private enterprises operating across multiple countries.

choices.text: A company that operates in multiple countries and integrates production and sales globally
choices.explanation: Multinational corporations maintain operations, factories, offices, or sales networks in multiple countries, often shifting production to low-wage regions while selling in high-income markets.

choices.text: A corporation that exports goods but maintains all production in its home country
choices.explanation: Export-only companies are not necessarily multinationals; multinationals specifically establish operations (production, headquarters, subsidiaries) in multiple countries.

choices.text: An international bank that loans money to developing nations
choices.explanation: International lending institutions like the World Bank are not multinational corporations; multinational corporations produce and sell goods or services across borders.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how multinational corporations have shaped global economic development

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "We are not the slaves of History, we are not necessarily chained to reproducing the world as it is. Those who profit from the current global order have a deep interest in persuading us that globalization is inevitable, that there is no alternative, that the market is simply the natural order of things. But markets are human creations. They can be shaped by political will." — Joseph Stiglitz, Globalization and Its Discontents, 2002

questions.question: Stiglitz's argument in the passage primarily challenges which widely held belief about globalization?

choices.text: The belief that free trade always benefits developing nations equally
choices.explanation: While Stiglitz critiques unequal benefits, his primary argument in this passage is about inevitability and the role of political agency in shaping globalization.

choices.text: The belief that globalization is a natural and inevitable process beyond human control
choices.explanation: Stiglitz directly challenges the 'TINA' (There Is No Alternative) ideology promoted by neoliberals, arguing that markets and globalization are political constructs that can be reformed through collective will.

choices.text: The belief that multinational corporations are harmful to host nations
choices.explanation: Stiglitz does not make a blanket claim about corporate harm here; his argument focuses on the ideology of inevitability, not corporate behavior specifically.

choices.text: The belief that international trade organizations are effective at reducing poverty
choices.explanation: Institutional effectiveness is not the passage's focus; the argument is about the political nature of global economic rules, not the effectiveness of specific institutions.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
In exchange for emergency loans during financial crises, the International Monetary Fund and World Bank required borrowing governments to implement specific policies: cutting government subsidies, privatizing state-owned enterprises, eliminating price controls, reducing public sector employment, and opening their markets to foreign goods and investment. Critics argued these policies increased poverty and inequality in the countries that implemented them.
— Adapted from a history of structural adjustment programs

questions.question: The 'Washington Consensus' of the 1980s and 1990s, promoted by the IMF and World Bank, prescribed which set of policies for developing nations seeking loans?

choices.text: Nationalization of key industries, land reform, and debt forgiveness
choices.explanation: Nationalization and land reform are socialist policies that the Washington Consensus explicitly opposed; debt relief was rarely part of initial IMF structural adjustment programs.

choices.text: Fiscal austerity, privatization, trade liberalization, and deregulation
choices.explanation: The Washington Consensus required borrowing nations to cut government spending, privatize state enterprises, open markets to foreign competition, and deregulate — policies critics argued devastated social services in poor nations.

choices.text: Increased social spending, import tariffs, and currency controls
choices.explanation: Increased social spending and import protection are the opposite of Washington Consensus prescriptions; these reflect Keynesian development economics.

choices.text: Military spending reductions and diplomatic normalization with rivals
choices.explanation: Military and diplomatic issues were separate from the Washington Consensus, which focused on economic policy conditions for international loans.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The world has never been more connected, and paradoxically never more threatened by the backlash against that connectivity. We see the rise of nationalism, populism, and religious fundamentalism as responses to the dislocations of global integration." — Historian's assessment, 2016

questions.question: The passage best supports which argument about globalization in the late 20th and early 21st centuries?

choices.text: Globalization has uniformly improved living standards worldwide, creating stability
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states that globalization has generated backlash and instability — the opposite of uniform improvement and stability.

choices.text: Economic integration has generated political and cultural counter-movements
choices.explanation: The passage argues that globalization's dislocations — job losses, cultural change, inequality — have fueled nationalist, populist, and religious fundamentalist movements as reactions against global integration.

choices.text: Religious fundamentalism is the primary cause of globalization's failures
choices.explanation: The passage presents religious fundamentalism as a response to globalization, not as its cause — the causal arrow points from globalization's dislocations to fundamentalism.

choices.text: Nationalism is incompatible with international trade and cooperation
choices.explanation: The passage describes nationalism as a response to globalization but does not argue that the two are inherently incompatible as systems.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has generated resistance and reform movements

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: China's rapid economic growth since 1978, often called the 'Chinese economic miracle,' was primarily based on which development strategy?

choices.text: Pure central planning with no foreign investment
choices.explanation: Pure central planning characterized the Maoist era; Deng Xiaoping's reforms explicitly opened China to foreign investment and market mechanisms, departing from the Maoist model.

choices.text: Export-led manufacturing growth, foreign direct investment, and Special Economic Zones
choices.explanation: Deng Xiaoping's reforms created Special Economic Zones attracting foreign investment, developed export-oriented manufacturing, and integrated China into global supply chains, driving unprecedented GDP growth.

choices.text: Oil and resource exports financing industrial development
choices.explanation: China's growth was based on manufacturing and exports, not oil revenues; this resource-export model better describes Gulf states and Russia.

choices.text: Western aid and Marshall Plan-style reconstruction
choices.explanation: China did not receive Marshall Plan aid; its development was internally driven through market reforms and integration into global supply chains.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how developing economies have participated in and been affected by globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
Tens of thousands of protesters descended on Seattle in November 1999 to disrupt the World Trade Organization ministerial conference. The protesters included labor unions worried about competition from low-wage countries, environmental groups concerned about relaxed regulations, farmers from developing nations who could not compete with subsidized Western agriculture, and activists who saw the WTO as an unaccountable institution serving corporate interests over democratic governance.
— Adapted from an account of the 1999 Seattle WTO protests

questions.question: The anti-globalization protests at the 1999 World Trade Organization ministerial conference in Seattle primarily reflected opposition to which concern?

choices.text: The WTO's failure to reduce tariff barriers effectively
choices.explanation: Protesters did not criticize the WTO for being insufficiently free-trade; they criticized free trade for harming workers, the environment, and developing nations.

choices.text: Trade liberalization's effects on labor rights, environmental standards, and inequality
choices.explanation: The 'Battle of Seattle' brought together labor unions, environmental activists, and development advocates who argued that WTO-driven trade liberalization benefited corporations at the expense of workers, ecosystems, and poor nations.

choices.text: The dominance of European nations in setting global trade rules
choices.explanation: Protesters did not focus on European dominance specifically; their critique was of corporate-driven globalization and its social and environmental costs.

choices.text: The exclusion of agricultural goods from international trade agreements
choices.explanation: Agricultural trade inclusion was a contested trade negotiation issue, but the Seattle protests were focused on broader concerns about globalization's human and environmental costs.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has generated resistance and reform movements

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
| Region | Share of Global Manufacturing Exports, 1980 | Share of Global Manufacturing Exports, 2010 |
|---|---|---|
| East Asia | 12% | 38% |
| North America | 22% | 14% |
| Western Europe | 45% | 28% |
| Latin America | 6% | 6% |
| Rest of World | 15% | 14% |

questions.question: Which conclusion is best supported by the data in the table?

choices.text: Latin America remained economically stagnant due to exclusion from global trade
choices.explanation: The table shows Latin America's share held constant at 6%, which may reflect stagnation relative to East Asia but does not establish that Latin America was excluded from trade.

choices.text: East Asia dramatically increased its share of global manufacturing exports while North America and Western Europe declined
choices.explanation: The data shows East Asia's share tripling from 12% to 38%, while North America fell from 22% to 14% and Western Europe from 45% to 28% — reflecting the shift of manufacturing to lower-wage Asian economies.

choices.text: Global manufacturing became more evenly distributed among all world regions
choices.explanation: The data shows increased concentration in East Asia rather than more even distribution — East Asia gained while most other regions held steady or declined.

choices.text: Western Europe maintained its dominant position in global manufacturing exports
choices.explanation: Western Europe's share fell significantly from 45% to 28%, losing its dominant position to East Asia's rise.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
The Maastricht Treaty of 1992 transformed the European Community into the European Union, creating a single internal market, establishing EU citizenship, and setting the framework for a common currency. When the euro was introduced in 1999, member nations surrendered control over their monetary policy to the European Central Bank. This represented an unprecedented transfer of sovereignty from nation-states to a supranational institution.
— Adapted from a history of European integration

questions.question: The European Union's creation as a single currency zone (Eurozone) in 1999 represented which aspect of globalization?

choices.text: Military integration to defend against external threats
choices.explanation: Military integration in Europe occurs through NATO, not the EU or Eurozone; the euro was specifically an economic and monetary project.

choices.text: Political fragmentation as nation-states rejected international institutions
choices.explanation: The Eurozone represents integration and pooling of sovereignty, the opposite of political fragmentation.

choices.text: Regional economic integration involving shared monetary policy and reduced transaction costs
choices.explanation: The euro eliminated currency exchange costs within the Eurozone, required members to cede national monetary policy to the European Central Bank, and deepened economic integration among member states.

choices.text: Cultural homogenization as European nations abandoned national identities
choices.explanation: The Eurozone was a monetary, not cultural, project; European nations maintained distinct languages, cultures, and national identities while sharing a currency.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how regional trade agreements have shaped global economic integration

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
On January 1, 1994, the same day that NAFTA went into effect, indigenous Maya farmers in Chiapas, Mexico, launched an armed uprising. Their manifesto declared that NAFTA was a death sentence for indigenous communities who would be unable to compete with subsidized American corn flooding Mexican markets. The Zapatistas demanded land reform, indigenous rights, and protection from the economic disruption of globalization.
— Adapted from an account of the Zapatista uprising

questions.question: The Zapatista uprising in Chiapas, Mexico (1994) was directly linked to globalization because the rebels explicitly protested which event occurring on the same day?

choices.text: Mexico's admission into the United Nations
choices.explanation: Mexico joined the UN in 1945, decades before the Zapatista uprising; the 1994 timing was not related to UN membership.

choices.text: The North American Free Trade Agreement taking effect
choices.explanation: The Zapatistas launched their uprising on January 1, 1994, the same day NAFTA took effect, explicitly framing it as a response to trade liberalization policies they argued would devastate indigenous farmers.

choices.text: The election of a new Mexican president
choices.explanation: While the Zapatistas opposed the ruling PRI party, the uprising's January 1 timing was specifically linked to NAFTA's implementation, not the presidential election cycle.

choices.text: Mexico's privatization of the national oil company PEMEX
choices.explanation: PEMEX was not privatized in 1994; the Zapatista uprising's specific January 1 timing was chosen to coincide with NAFTA's implementation as a symbolic protest against free trade.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has generated resistance and reform movements

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The 'race to the bottom' phenomenon in globalization refers to which dynamic?

choices.text: Declining literacy rates in developing nations as manufacturing replaces education
choices.explanation: Literacy rates are not the 'race to the bottom'; this concept specifically refers to competitive lowering of regulatory and labor standards.

choices.text: Competition among nations to lower wages, environmental standards, and labor protections to attract foreign investment
choices.explanation: The 'race to the bottom' describes how countries compete for multinational investment by weakening labor laws, environmental regulations, and wages — creating a competitive spiral of deteriorating standards.

choices.text: The tendency of commodity prices to fall as global supply increases
choices.explanation: Commodity price dynamics are a separate economic phenomenon from the 'race to the bottom,' which specifically refers to competitive deregulation.

choices.text: The decline of traditional industries as technology replaces manual labor
choices.explanation: Technological displacement of labor is a distinct phenomenon from the race to the bottom, which is about regulatory competition between countries for investment.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how multinational corporations have shaped global economic development

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "In the 1980s and 1990s, structural adjustment programs required African governments to cut spending on health, education, and infrastructure as conditions for receiving IMF and World Bank loans. The result in many countries was a sharp deterioration in public services precisely when those services were most needed to support development." — Development economist's critique, 2000

questions.question: The passage most directly supports which critique of international financial institutions during this period?

choices.text: International institutions provided insufficient loans for African nations' military modernization
choices.explanation: Military spending is not the passage's focus; the critique centers on the social effects of mandatory austerity in health, education, and infrastructure.

choices.text: Loan conditions that required austerity undermined the development goals they were intended to promote
choices.explanation: The passage argues structural adjustment's austerity requirements cut the very public investments (health, education, infrastructure) that are prerequisites for sustainable development, creating a self-defeating cycle.

choices.text: African governments misused international loans for corruption rather than development
choices.explanation: The passage blames the loan conditions imposed by the IMF and World Bank, not African government corruption, for the deterioration in public services.

choices.text: Too much foreign aid created dependency that undermined African self-sufficiency
choices.explanation: The 'aid dependency' critique is a different argument; the passage specifically criticizes the austerity conditions attached to loans, not aid dependency.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Kyoto Protocol (1997) represented an attempt to address which global challenge through international cooperation?

choices.text: Nuclear weapons proliferation among developing nations
choices.explanation: Nuclear non-proliferation is governed by the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT); the Kyoto Protocol specifically addressed climate change and greenhouse gas emissions.

choices.text: Anthropogenic climate change through binding limits on greenhouse gas emissions
choices.explanation: The Kyoto Protocol committed developed nations to binding reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, representing the first major international treaty specifically targeting human-caused climate change.

choices.text: Transnational terrorism through intelligence-sharing agreements
choices.explanation: Counterterrorism cooperation is handled through separate bilateral and multilateral security agreements; the Kyoto Protocol was an environmental treaty.

choices.text: Deforestation and biodiversity loss in tropical regions
choices.explanation: Biodiversity is addressed by the Convention on Biological Diversity; the Kyoto Protocol focused specifically on greenhouse gas emissions and climate change.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected the natural environment

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks represented a significant moment in globalization history primarily because they demonstrated which aspect of an interconnected world?

choices.text: The ability of international institutions to respond effectively to transnational threats
choices.explanation: The 9/11 attacks revealed the limitations of institutions in preventing transnational threats; the UN did not prevent the attacks and the response was largely unilateral (US-led).

choices.text: Non-state actors could use global networks and technologies to threaten the most powerful states
choices.explanation: Al-Qaeda used global banking networks, communication technologies, airline routes, and transnational recruitment to plan and execute attacks — demonstrating that globalization's open networks could be weaponized by non-state actors.

choices.text: Economic globalization could be permanently halted by security concerns
choices.explanation: While 9/11 increased security measures, economic globalization continued — it was not permanently halted; global trade and investment resumed within months.

choices.text: State-sponsored terrorism had become the primary threat to international stability
choices.explanation: Al-Qaeda was a non-state actor, not state-sponsored; the 9/11 attacks are specifically significant for demonstrating non-state actors' global capabilities.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected political developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The concept of 'global commodity chains' best describes which aspect of modern globalization?

choices.text: The dominance of a single nation in controlling world trade through military power
choices.explanation: Global commodity chains describe distributed production networks, not single-nation control; they represent the dispersal of production across many countries, not concentration.

choices.text: The fragmentation of production across multiple countries in an integrated global production network
choices.explanation: Commodity chains describe how a single product (like a smartphone or garment) involves raw materials, manufacturing, assembly, marketing, and retail occurring in different countries — integrating global production.

choices.text: The standardization of consumer goods through international trademark agreements
choices.explanation: Trademark and intellectual property agreements are separate legal mechanisms; commodity chains refer to the geographic distribution of production processes, not product standardization.

choices.text: The creation of cartels among commodity-producing nations to control prices
choices.explanation: Producer cartels like OPEC are a different phenomenon; global commodity chains describe production networks, not price-control arrangements.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how multinational corporations have shaped global economic development

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The BRICS countries — Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa — represent a challenge to Western-dominated global governance. Their combined GDP has grown from roughly 8% of global output in 1990 to over 30% by 2015, and they have called for reform of institutions like the IMF and UN Security Council to better reflect the new distribution of global economic power." — International relations analysis, 2016

questions.question: Which long-term historical development does the formation of BRICS most directly continue?

choices.text: The Non-Aligned Movement's challenge to Cold War bipolarity
choices.explanation: Like the Non-Aligned Movement (1955 Bandung), BRICS represents a coalition of major developing-world powers challenging the dominance of Western institutions and seeking greater voice in global governance — continuing a tradition of Global South assertiveness.

choices.text: The Cold War pattern of military alliances against Western powers
choices.explanation: BRICS is not a military alliance; it is an economic and diplomatic grouping, more analogous to the Non-Aligned Movement than to Warsaw Pact-style military alignment.

choices.text: The colonial-era pattern of resource extraction from developing nations
choices.explanation: BRICS nations are challenging Western economic dominance, not reproducing colonial patterns; the passage explicitly describes their growing economic power and demands for institutional reform.

choices.text: The post-WWII creation of international institutions to maintain peace
choices.explanation: The post-WWII institutions (UN, IMF, World Bank) were created by Western powers; BRICS is explicitly calling for reform of those institutions, not recreating them.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how developing economies have participated in and been affected by globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The HIV/AIDS pandemic, which killed over 25 million people between 1981 and 2006, demonstrated which consequence of globalization?

choices.text: Disease could spread globally with unprecedented speed through interconnected travel and migration networks
choices.explanation: HIV/AIDS spread from Central Africa globally through interconnected travel, migration, and commercial networks — demonstrating that globalization's mobility of people could rapidly diffuse disease across all borders.

choices.text: International institutions were effective at rapidly containing novel infectious diseases
choices.explanation: The AIDS pandemic revealed failures of international health response — stigma, slow research funding, and pharmaceutical access barriers delayed effective responses for decades.

choices.text: Disease primarily affected wealthy nations that were more integrated into global networks
choices.explanation: HIV/AIDS disproportionately devastated Sub-Saharan Africa's poorest populations; wealthy nations had far better access to antiretroviral drugs that reduced mortality.

choices.text: Technological globalization eliminated geographic barriers to medical research cooperation
choices.explanation: While research cooperation occurred, intellectual property barriers and pharmaceutical pricing actually impeded technology transfer to poor nations — the HIV/AIDS crisis highlighted failures of technology sharing, not its success.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected human health and population

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "Globalization is not new. What distinguishes the current era from the earlier globalizations of the 16th and 19th centuries is not the fact of interconnection but its intensity, speed, and the degree to which it penetrates into the domestic political economy of every nation on earth. Previous eras had trading posts; today's multinationals reshape the legal frameworks of sovereign states." — Historian's comparative analysis, 2004

questions.question: The historian's argument most directly challenges which commonly held view about contemporary globalization?

choices.text: The view that modern globalization is entirely unprecedented with no historical parallels
choices.explanation: The historian argues that globalization has historical antecedents (16th-century trade networks, 19th-century imperialism) and what is distinctive is not the fact of integration but its depth and speed — directly challenging 'unprecedented' claims.

choices.text: The view that multinational corporations are beneficial to developing nations
choices.explanation: The passage is concerned with comparing historical eras of globalization, not with evaluating corporate impact on development — the focus is on periodization and what distinguishes current globalization.

choices.text: The view that the nation-state has become irrelevant in the globalized world
choices.explanation: While the passage notes that multinationals influence sovereign legal frameworks, it does not argue that nation-states are irrelevant; the claim about historical parallels is the passage's primary argument.

choices.text: The view that international trade creates mutual benefit among all participating nations
choices.explanation: Trade's distributional effects are not the passage's subject; the historian is making a periodization argument about what distinguishes contemporary globalization from earlier forms.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the development of globalization after the Cold War

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Critics argue that the post-Cold War 'unipolar moment' of US dominance created conditions for which long-term global development?

choices.text: The permanent pacification of international relations as liberal democracy spread globally
choices.explanation: The 'democratic peace' hypothesis overestimated the pacifying effects of liberal democracy; the post-Cold War era saw significant interstate and civil conflicts, and many democracies later experienced democratic backsliding.

choices.text: The rise of authoritarian regional powers challenging US-led international order
choices.explanation: Critics argue that the unipolar moment's overreach (Iraq War, Afghanistan), combined with economic globalization's dislocations, created conditions for the rise of China, Russia's revanchism, and regional powers challenging US hegemony by the 2010s.

choices.text: The elimination of nuclear weapons through successful disarmament agreements
choices.explanation: Nuclear disarmament was not achieved; while START treaties reduced US-Russian arsenals, proliferation continued (India, Pakistan, North Korea) and the unipolar moment did not produce denuclearization.

choices.text: The transformation of the UN into an effective world government with enforcement powers
choices.explanation: The UN did not acquire effective enforcement powers; the unipolar era was characterized by US bypassing UN processes (Kosovo, Iraq) rather than strengthening multilateral institutions.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected political developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The maquiladora system along the US-Mexico border exemplifies the contradictions of export-led development. These factories create employment and foreign exchange earnings while simultaneously anchoring workers in low-wage labor with limited upward mobility, repatriating profits to foreign shareholders, and creating environmental degradation in regions with weak regulatory enforcement." — Development studies analysis, 1998

questions.question: The maquiladora system described in the passage most closely parallels which earlier historical pattern?

choices.text: The enclave economy model in colonial Africa and Asia, where foreign investment extracted value while limiting local economic development
choices.explanation: Maquiladoras parallel colonial enclave economies (mines, plantations) that generated foreign exchange and employed local workers while repatriating profits abroad, limiting linkages to the broader domestic economy — a continuity in dependent development patterns.

choices.text: The Japanese Meiji industrialization model, where state-directed development built competitive domestic industries
choices.explanation: Meiji industrialization built nationally owned industries with strong state guidance and profit retention domestically; maquiladoras are foreign-owned with profit repatriation — the opposite development model.

choices.text: The Soviet command economy model, where state planning directed industrial development
choices.explanation: Maquiladoras represent market-driven, foreign-led manufacturing integration — not state planning; Soviet-style development kept profits within the national economy under state control.

choices.text: The European Marshall Plan model, where foreign investment built self-sustaining development
choices.explanation: The Marshall Plan aimed at building self-sustaining economies with technology transfer; maquiladoras are criticized precisely for failing to build such self-sustaining development.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how multinational corporations have shaped global economic development

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The 2008 global financial crisis, which originated in US subprime mortgage markets, most directly demonstrated which feature of contemporary economic globalization?

choices.text: The vulnerability of tightly integrated global financial markets to systemic contagion
choices.explanation: The 2008 crisis spread from US mortgage markets to global credit markets in weeks, demonstrating that financial integration had created interconnected fragility where a crisis in one sector or country rapidly became a global recession — the 'too connected to fail' problem.

choices.text: The effectiveness of international financial institutions in preventing economic crises
choices.explanation: The 2008 crisis revealed failures of regulatory oversight and IMF early-warning systems; institutions did not prevent the crisis and struggled to coordinate effective responses.

choices.text: The success of neoliberal financial deregulation in maximizing global economic growth
choices.explanation: The 2008 crisis is widely interpreted as a consequence of financial deregulation, not its success; it led to calls for greater financial regulation and oversight.

choices.text: The declining importance of the United States in the global economy
choices.explanation: The fact that a US financial crisis spread globally demonstrates US centrality to the world economy, not its declining importance.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "Between 1990 and 2015, the number of people living in extreme poverty (under $1.90/day) fell from 1.9 billion to 700 million. However, income inequality within most nations increased sharply during this same period. The Gini coefficient for China rose from 0.30 in 1980 to 0.47 in 2010; in the United States it rose from 0.35 to 0.41. Globally, the top 1% captured 27% of all income growth between 1980 and 2016." — World Bank/Piketty data synthesis, 2017

questions.question: The data in the passage best supports which conclusion about the relationship between globalization and inequality?

choices.text: Globalization has been entirely harmful to human welfare and should be reversed
choices.explanation: The data shows significant poverty reduction (1.2 billion people lifted from extreme poverty), indicating globalization produced some welfare gains — a blanket reversal argument is not supported by the passage's evidence.

choices.text: Globalization reduced between-country inequality while increasing within-country inequality, with gains concentrated at the top
choices.explanation: The passage shows falling extreme poverty (reducing between-country gaps) alongside rising domestic Gini coefficients and top-1% income concentration — capturing the paradox that globalization created simultaneous convergence at the international level and divergence within societies.

choices.text: Rising inequality is primarily a problem in China and has not affected Western nations significantly
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly shows US Gini rising from 0.35 to 0.41, and notes that the top 1% globally captured 27% of growth — inequality is not limited to China.

choices.text: International poverty reduction programs, not trade liberalization, account for the decrease in extreme poverty
choices.explanation: The passage does not attribute poverty reduction specifically to aid programs vs. trade; historians generally attribute the bulk of poverty reduction to China's export-led growth within globalized markets, not foreign aid.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Scholars who argue that globalization represents a continuation of earlier imperialism point to which structural continuity?

choices.text: Wealthy nations continue to maintain formal colonial administrations over developing nations
choices.explanation: Formal colonialism ended with decolonization; neo-imperialism arguments focus on informal economic power, not formal colonial administration.

choices.text: Core-periphery relationships in which wealthy nations extract value from developing nations through unequal exchange
choices.explanation: World-systems theorists argue that contemporary globalization reproduces the colonial-era core-periphery structure through terms of trade, intellectual property rules, debt conditionality, and financial flows that structurally disadvantage peripheral nations.

choices.text: Military conquest and direct occupation of developing nations by wealthy states
choices.explanation: While military interventions occur, neo-imperialism arguments in globalization scholarship focus on economic mechanisms (trade rules, financial flows, conditionality) rather than military conquest.

choices.text: The cultural destruction of indigenous societies through forced religious conversion
choices.explanation: Religious conversion was a feature of early modern colonialism; contemporary imperialism arguments focus on economic and structural power, not missionary religious conversion.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how developing economies have participated in and been affected by globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Arab Spring of 2010–2012 demonstrated both the empowering and destabilizing potential of digital communication technologies. Social media networks enabled unprecedented cross-border coordination among activists, while also serving as tools for government surveillance. The revolutions that succeeded — Tunisia — did so through sustained organizing; those that failed or devolved into civil war — Libya, Syria, Yemen — suggest that social media could catalyze mobilization but could not substitute for durable political institutions." — Political scientist's analysis, 2014

questions.question: Which broader conclusion about the relationship between technology and political change does the passage most strongly support?

choices.text: Digital communication technology is the primary driver of democratic revolution in the 21st century
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly warns against technological determinism — Tunisia succeeded through 'sustained organizing,' not technology alone; the technology catalyzed but did not substitute for political institutions.

choices.text: Technology can enable new forms of political mobilization but cannot replace the structural conditions necessary for sustainable democratic change
choices.explanation: The passage's core argument is that social media catalyzed the Arab Spring but could not substitute for 'durable political institutions' — technology is a tool, not a deterministic driver of specific political outcomes.

choices.text: Government surveillance technology has made democratic revolution impossible in the digital age
choices.explanation: The passage notes surveillance as a concern but does not conclude that revolution is impossible — Tunisia successfully transitioned despite surveillance capabilities.

choices.text: Social media primarily benefits authoritarian governments by enabling mass surveillance of dissidents
choices.explanation: The passage presents social media as having dual effects — both empowering activists and enabling surveillance — not primarily as a tool of authoritarian control.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how technology has shaped globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Amartya Sen's capability approach to development, which influenced the UN Human Development Index, challenged traditional development economics by arguing that development should be measured by which criterion?

choices.text: GDP per capita growth rates, which best indicate rising living standards
choices.explanation: Sen explicitly challenged GDP-centric development metrics as inadequate; GDP measures economic output but not whether people can actually live fulfilling lives.

choices.text: The expansion of human freedoms and capabilities — health, education, and political participation
choices.explanation: Sen argued development means expanding what people can be and do — their 'capabilities' — including living long and healthy lives, being educated, and having political voice; this framework produced the Human Development Index (HDI) as an alternative to GDP.

choices.text: Military security and national sovereignty as preconditions for all other development
choices.explanation: Sen's capability approach is centered on human freedoms, not national security; while stability matters, his framework prioritizes individual human flourishing over state-security metrics.

choices.text: Trade balance and export competitiveness as indicators of sustainable economic growth
choices.explanation: Trade metrics are macroeconomic indicators; Sen's capability approach is fundamentally concerned with individual human welfare and freedom, not national trade performance.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how developing economies have participated in and been affected by globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content:
| Phenomenon | Pre-1914 | 1945–1990 | 1990–Present |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trade as % of world GDP | 14% | 8% | 25% |
| Capital flows (relative to GDP) | High | Restricted | Very High |
| Migration (millions/decade) | 10m+ | ~3m | ~20m+ |
| Communications technology | Telegraph, telephone | Radio, TV, satellite | Internet, mobile |
| Dominant governing mechanism | Empires, bilateral treaties | Bretton Woods, UN | WTO, regional blocs, NGOs |

questions.question: Which conclusion about historical patterns of globalization is best supported by the data in the table?

choices.text: Contemporary globalization is unique in history and has no meaningful parallels in earlier periods
choices.explanation: The table shows that pre-1914 trade integration was substantial (14% of GDP) and migration exceeded early Cold War levels — directly refuting claims of complete historical uniqueness.

choices.text: The Cold War era represented a period of deglobalization relative to both earlier and later periods
choices.explanation: The 1945–1990 data shows trade falling to 8% of GDP, capital flows restricted, and migration at a fraction of other periods — indicating that the Cold War era was a relative deglobalization between the first globalization (pre-1914) and the second (post-1990).

choices.text: Migration has continuously increased throughout modern history with no disruptions
choices.explanation: The table shows a significant drop in migration from 10m+ per decade pre-1914 to ~3m during 1945–1990 — demonstrating disruption, not continuous increase.

choices.text: Capital markets have been tightly restricted throughout modern history except for brief periods
choices.explanation: The table shows that capital flows were high pre-1914, restricted during the Cold War, then very high post-1990 — capital liberalization was the exception, then the norm by the 2000s, not consistently restricted.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the development of globalization after the Cold War

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The concept of 'brain drain' in the context of globalization refers to which phenomenon?

choices.text: The loss of cultural traditions as global media standardizes local cultures
choices.explanation: Cultural homogenization through media is a distinct phenomenon — 'brain drain' specifically refers to the migration of skilled and educated workers, not cultural change.

choices.text: The migration of highly educated and skilled workers from developing to developed nations, depriving origin countries of human capital
choices.explanation: Brain drain occurs when doctors, engineers, and educated professionals emigrate from low-wage developing nations to high-wage developed nations, depriving origin countries of the human capital needed for development.

choices.text: The deskilling of workers in developing nations as automation replaces skilled labor
choices.explanation: Labor deskilling through automation is a distinct economic process; brain drain refers to the geographic migration of skilled people, not technological displacement.

choices.text: The dominance of Western educational models that undermine indigenous knowledge systems
choices.explanation: Western educational hegemony is a separate cultural critique; brain drain specifically addresses the migration flow of skilled workers and its economic consequences for sending nations.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected human health and population

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The European Union began as the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1957, primarily as a project to prevent which recurring historical problem?

choices.text: Colonial competition among European powers for African territories
choices.explanation: By 1957, decolonization was well underway; the EEC's founding purpose was European economic integration to prevent intra-European warfare, not colonial competition.

choices.text: Devastating inter-European wars that had twice destroyed the continent in the 20th century
choices.explanation: The EEC's founders — particularly France and Germany — intended economic integration to make war between European powers not just unlikely but materially impossible through interdependence.

choices.text: Soviet expansion into Western European economies
choices.explanation: While the Cold War context mattered, NATO addressed the military dimension of Soviet expansion; the EEC was specifically designed to integrate European economies and prevent intra-European conflict.

choices.text: American economic dominance of European markets after World War II
choices.explanation: While some Europeans were concerned about US economic influence, the EEC's founding rationale was preventing intra-European war through integration, not countering American economic power.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how regional trade agreements have shaped global economic integration

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'outsourcing' as a feature of contemporary globalization?

choices.text: Government contracts given to private firms for formerly public services
choices.explanation: Government privatization of services is a related but distinct phenomenon; outsourcing in the globalization context specifically refers to international relocation of business functions to lower-cost countries.

choices.text: The relocation of business processes or manufacturing to foreign countries with lower labor costs
choices.explanation: Outsourcing involves companies moving functions like manufacturing, customer service, or software development to countries (India, China, Philippines) with lower wages, reducing costs while maintaining the product/service for home markets.

choices.text: The sale of natural resources by developing nations to foreign buyers
choices.explanation: Resource export is a different trade pattern; outsourcing refers to the deliberate relocation of business activities by firms to reduce costs, not resource extraction.

choices.text: The practice of hiring temporary workers instead of permanent employees
choices.explanation: Temporary employment (gig economy) is a distinct labor market phenomenon; outsourcing specifically involves cross-border movement of business functions.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how multinational corporations have shaped global economic development

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: India's rapid growth in information technology services in the 1990s–2000s, often called the 'Indian IT miracle,' was facilitated by which combination of factors?

choices.text: Large low-wage manufacturing workforce and proximity to US markets
choices.explanation: India's IT success was based on a large English-speaking, technically educated workforce in services, not on manufacturing proximity or geographic location.

choices.text: English-language proficiency, large technically educated workforce, time zone advantages for US outsourcing, and internet connectivity
choices.explanation: India's IT sector grew through the combination of English-language skills (colonial legacy), large engineering graduate output, time zone overlap enabling 24-hour operations for US clients, and the internet enabling remote service delivery.

choices.text: State ownership of technology firms and government-directed industrial policy
choices.explanation: India's IT boom was driven by private sector firms (Infosys, TCS, Wipro) and economic liberalization in 1991, not state ownership; though government investment in technical education (IITs) contributed to the workforce.

choices.text: Natural resource wealth funding technical education and infrastructure investment
choices.explanation: India is not a major resource exporter; the IT sector developed through human capital investment (IIT system, English education) and liberalization, not resource revenues.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how developing economies have participated in and been affected by globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "Fast fashion — the model of rapidly producing inexpensive, disposable clothing through global supply chains — exemplifies the paradoxes of globalization. It democratizes fashion by making stylish clothing affordable to working-class consumers in wealthy nations while simultaneously depending on precarious, low-wage labor in Bangladesh, Vietnam, and Cambodia, and generating 92 million tons of textile waste annually." — Environmental economist, 2019

questions.question: Which analytical framework best explains the dynamics described in the fast fashion passage?

choices.text: Dependency theory — wealthy nations extract value from peripheral nations through unequal trade
choices.explanation: Fast fashion exemplifies dependency theory's core argument: core (wealthy) consumers benefit from low prices generated by exploiting peripheral (developing) labor and environments, reproducing unequal exchange relationships that prevent peripheral nations from capturing full value of their inputs.

choices.text: Modernization theory — manufacturing integration drives development in poor nations
choices.explanation: Modernization theory would predict that garment industry integration moves Bangladesh toward development; the passage emphasizes 'precarious, low-wage labor' and environmental costs, suggesting the development dividend is limited.

choices.text: Comparative advantage theory — all parties gain from specialization in production
choices.explanation: Comparative advantage predicts mutual gains; the passage highlights distributional asymmetries — consumer benefits in wealthy nations vs. labor exploitation and environmental costs in producing nations — challenging the mutual-gain framing.

choices.text: Mercantilism — nations compete for positive trade balances through export promotion
choices.explanation: Mercantilism focuses on state trade policy for national advantage; fast fashion is driven by corporate supply chains and consumer demand, not state mercantilist competition.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how multinational corporations have shaped global economic development

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Millennium Development Goals (MDGs), established by the UN in 2000, and their successor Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs, 2015) represented which approach to global inequality?

choices.text: Market-only solutions where economic growth alone would eliminate poverty and disease
choices.explanation: Both MDGs and SDGs explicitly called for targeted international aid, debt relief, and policy coordination beyond free markets; they represent a mixed public-private approach, not pure market solutions.

choices.text: Multilateral commitment to specific measurable targets for poverty reduction, health, education, and environmental sustainability
choices.explanation: MDGs and SDGs established specific, time-bound, measurable targets (reduce child mortality by two-thirds, achieve universal primary education, etc.) with shared international accountability — a normative framework for global development cooperation.

choices.text: Military intervention to remove governments that fail to meet development benchmarks
choices.explanation: Neither the MDGs nor SDGs included military enforcement mechanisms; they were voluntary commitments supported by international peer pressure and aid incentives.

choices.text: Replacement of national sovereignty with UN governance over developing nations' economic policies
choices.explanation: MDGs and SDGs respect national sovereignty; they set aspirational targets and provide frameworks but do not supersede national policy authority.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has generated resistance and reform movements

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The 'resource curse' hypothesis in development economics argues that nations with large natural resource (oil, minerals) endowments often experience which paradoxical outcome?

choices.text: Accelerated democratization as resource revenues fund education and civic institutions
choices.explanation: The resource curse argues the opposite — resource wealth often funds authoritarian regimes that buy loyalty rather than invest in education and civic development.

choices.text: Slower economic development, weaker institutions, and more authoritarian governance than resource-poor nations
choices.explanation: Resource-rich nations often exhibit 'Dutch Disease' (resource exports driving up currency and killing other industries), rent-seeking by elites, weak tax-based state-citizen accountability, and authoritarian stability through resource revenue distribution.

choices.text: Rapid industrialization funded by resource revenues leading to diversified economies
choices.explanation: While some resource revenues can fund diversification (e.g., Norway), the resource curse hypothesis identifies the more common pattern of failing to diversify — dependence on resources that inhibits industrial development.

choices.text: Rapid population growth that overwhelms resource revenue gains
choices.explanation: Population dynamics are a secondary concern in resource curse analysis; the core argument is about institutional and economic distortions from resource dependence, not demographic factors.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how developing economies have participated in and been affected by globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Remittances — money sent by migrant workers back to their home countries — have become a significant development finance mechanism. Which statement best describes their significance?

choices.text: Remittances are primarily used for luxury consumption and contribute little to economic development
choices.explanation: Studies show significant portions of remittances fund education, healthcare, and small business investment in origin communities, not primarily luxury consumption.

choices.text: Remittances in many developing nations exceed official development aid and direct foreign investment
choices.explanation: Globally, remittances (~$600 billion/year by 2015) exceed official development aid and in nations like the Philippines, Mexico, and Guatemala amount to 10–20% of GDP — often the largest source of foreign exchange.

choices.text: Remittances primarily flow from developing nations to wealthy nations as debt repayments
choices.explanation: Remittances flow from migrants (in wealthy countries) back to their origin countries (developing nations) — the opposite direction from debt repayments.

choices.text: International institutions like the World Bank control remittance flows to ensure they support development goals
choices.explanation: Remittances are private transfers between individuals and families; no international institution controls these flows, which is partly why they are seen as more effective than aid — they reach households directly.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected human health and population

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Paris Agreement of 2015 marked a significant departure from the Kyoto Protocol's approach. Rather than imposing binding emission reduction targets on developed nations, Paris allowed each nation to set its own 'nationally determined contributions' and relied on transparency, peer pressure, and periodic reviews to drive ambition upward. Supporters called it pragmatic; critics called it inadequate." — Climate policy analyst, 2016

questions.question: The shift from Kyoto to Paris reflected which tension in global governance?

choices.text: The tension between sovereignty and effective multilateral action on global public goods
choices.explanation: Kyoto's binding targets were rejected by the US Senate and not accepted by major developing emitters; Paris accommodated sovereignty by allowing voluntary pledges, sacrificing binding enforcement for broader participation — illustrating the fundamental tension between state sovereignty and effective global governance.

choices.text: The tension between developed and developing nations over technology transfer payments
choices.explanation: While technology and finance transfers were negotiated in Paris, the structural shift from binding to voluntary targets reflects the sovereignty-effectiveness tension, not primarily a finance dispute.

choices.text: The tension between scientific consensus and political populism in climate policy
choices.explanation: Scientific consensus on climate is not the primary structural issue explaining the Kyoto-to-Paris shift; the key change was moving from binding to voluntary targets to achieve broader participation.

choices.text: The tension between large emitters seeking maximum targets and small nations seeking minimal commitments
choices.explanation: Small island states were among the most vocal advocates for stringent targets; the Paris framework's voluntary structure was designed to accommodate large emitters (US, China) who resisted binding multilateral commitments.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected the natural environment

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Scholars debate whether globalization promotes 'cultural homogenization' or 'hybridization.' Which argument best supports the hybridization thesis?

choices.text: Global media and brand culture have produced identical consumer preferences in all societies
choices.explanation: Uniform consumer preferences would support the homogenization thesis, not hybridization; hybridization argues for unique local adaptations, not standardization.

choices.text: Local cultures actively adopt, adapt, and blend global influences creating unique hybrid cultural forms
choices.explanation: Hybridization (Bhabha, García Canclini) argues that local cultures are not passively overwhelmed by global flows but actively appropriate and reinterpret them — creating K-pop, Afrobeats, Latin fusion cuisine — new hybrid forms rather than simple Westernization.

choices.text: Western languages have replaced all indigenous languages in globalized nations
choices.explanation: Language replacement would support homogenization; hybridization theorists note that most societies maintain multiple languages and adapt foreign linguistic elements rather than simply replacing local languages.

choices.text: Global trade has destroyed traditional artisanal production in all developing nations
choices.explanation: While some traditional crafts have declined, many persist and even flourish as global 'world music,' craft, and tourism markets create demand; the hybridization thesis emphasizes adaptation, not simple destruction.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected culture and society

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "Religious fundamentalism — whether Christian, Islamic, Hindu, or Jewish — shares a common structural feature across contexts: it represents a reaction to the perceived moral disorder, economic displacement, and cultural threat posed by secular modernity and globalization. Fundamentalists do not reject modernity's technologies but seek to deploy them in service of pre-modern sacred orders." — Sociologist of religion, 2001

questions.question: Which historical parallel best illuminates the dynamic described in the passage?

choices.text: The Protestant Reformation's use of printing press technology to challenge Catholic institutional authority
choices.explanation: While the Reformation used new technology for religious purposes, the parallel in the passage is about reactions to modernization's dislocations — not technological democratization of religious authority.

choices.text: Anti-colonial nationalism movements that adopted Western organizational forms while asserting cultural independence
choices.explanation: Anti-colonial nationalists (Gandhi, Ho Chi Minh) adopted modern organizational tools (political parties, newspapers, railways) while asserting non-Western cultural and civilizational values — paralleling the passage's description of fundamentalists using modern technologies in service of pre-modern sacred orders.

choices.text: The Scientific Revolution's challenge to religious authority through empirical methods
choices.explanation: The Scientific Revolution was a source of secularizing modernity, not a reaction to it; the passage describes reactions against secular modernity, not its origins.

choices.text: The Cold War arms race as both superpowers adopted similar technological means for competing ideological ends
choices.explanation: The arms race was an interstate geopolitical competition, not a cultural reaction to modernization's dislocations; the passage is about cultural responses to globalization's perceived threats to sacred orders.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has generated resistance and reform movements

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following is the most accurate description of a 'non-governmental organization' (NGO) in the context of globalization?

choices.text: A corporation that operates in multiple countries without government oversight
choices.explanation: Corporations are profit-driven private sector entities; NGOs are non-profit civil society organizations distinct from both governments and corporations.

choices.text: A private, nonprofit organization that operates independently of governments to address social, environmental, or humanitarian issues
choices.explanation: NGOs like Amnesty International, Doctors Without Borders, and Greenpeace are independent civil society organizations that advocate for human rights, environmental protection, or humanitarian causes outside government structures.

choices.text: An intergovernmental body created by treaty among member states
choices.explanation: Intergovernmental organizations (IGOs) like the UN or WHO are created by and accountable to member states; NGOs are independent of governments.

choices.text: A domestic lobbying group that influences national government policy
choices.explanation: While some NGOs engage in advocacy, the key feature distinguishing NGOs is their international civil society character and independence from governments, not domestic lobbying.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has generated resistance and reform movements

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The 'digital divide' in the context of globalization refers to which inequality?

choices.text: Unequal access to digital technologies between wealthy and poor nations and populations
choices.explanation: The digital divide describes how internet access, smartphones, and digital skills are concentrated in wealthy nations and upper-income populations, potentially deepening existing inequalities as digital connectivity becomes essential for economic participation.

choices.text: Disagreements between technology corporations about intellectual property rights
choices.explanation: Corporate IP disputes are a separate legal issue; the digital divide refers to societal access inequality, not corporate competition.

choices.text: The gap between military and civilian technology capabilities in developed nations
choices.explanation: Military-civilian technology gaps are a distinct concern; the digital divide specifically refers to unequal public access to digital technologies between wealthy and poor groups.

choices.text: Government censorship of internet content in authoritarian nations
choices.explanation: Internet censorship (the 'Great Firewall' of China, etc.) is a distinct issue from the digital divide, which refers to access inequality regardless of censorship.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how technology has shaped globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, and Hong Kong — the 'Asian Tigers' — achieved rapid industrialization primarily through which development model?

choices.text: Import-substitution industrialization with high tariffs protecting domestic industries
choices.explanation: The Asian Tigers are the classic example of export-led growth, not import substitution; Latin American nations like Brazil and Mexico pursued import substitution in the same era with less success.

choices.text: Export-led manufacturing with strong state guidance, targeted industrial policy, and investment in education
choices.explanation: The Asian Tiger model combined export orientation with developmental state features: strategic industrial targeting, forced savings, heavy investment in education and infrastructure, and disciplined labor markets — creating competitive export manufacturing industries.

choices.text: Oil revenue recycling through sovereign wealth funds investing in foreign assets
choices.explanation: None of the Asian Tigers are major oil exporters; their development was based on manufacturing exports and human capital, not resource revenues.

choices.text: Agricultural modernization and Green Revolution technologies funding industrial transition
choices.explanation: While agricultural change preceded industrialization in some cases, the Asian Tiger model's core dynamic was state-guided export manufacturing and human capital investment, not agricultural surplus funding industry.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how developing economies have participated in and been affected by globalization

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The growth of global cities — London, New York, Tokyo, Shanghai — represents a spatial concentration of globalization's benefits and contradictions. These cities host the headquarters of multinational corporations, international financial markets, and the global creative class, while also hosting large immigrant populations doing low-wage service work. They have more in common with each other than with their surrounding national hinterlands." — Saskia Sassen, adapted

questions.question: Sassen's analysis of global cities most directly illustrates which broader phenomenon of contemporary globalization?

choices.text: The declining importance of geography in economic activity as digital technology transcends space
choices.explanation: Sassen's global cities analysis explicitly emphasizes the spatial concentration of globalization in specific cities — arguing geography matters more than ever as key nodes in global networks.

choices.text: The deepening of inequalities between cosmopolitan elites and low-wage service workers within the same geographic spaces
choices.explanation: Sassen identifies global cities as sites of extreme polarization — finance executives and immigrant domestic workers coexist in the same city — illustrating how globalization produces simultaneous concentration of wealth and concentration of precarious low-wage work.

choices.text: The strengthening of national identities as cities become centers of cultural resistance
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly notes global cities have more in common with each other across nations than with their national hinterlands — emphasizing cosmopolitan rather than national identity.

choices.text: The success of global urbanization in lifting rural migrants into middle-class prosperity
choices.explanation: The passage emphasizes immigrants performing 'low-wage service work,' not middle-class mobility; Sassen's analysis is about polarization, not upward mobility.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how economic changes affected political and social developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Brexit vote (2016) and election of Donald Trump (2016) have been analyzed as expressions of similar underlying forces. Which explanation best captures the common structural factor?

choices.text: Both reflected concerns about immigration and cultural change among populations left behind by globalization's economic dislocations
choices.explanation: Both movements drew heavily from working-class voters in deindustrialized regions who experienced job losses from trade and automation, combined with cultural anxiety about immigration and rapid demographic change — illustrating the political backlash against globalization's distributional effects.

choices.text: Both were driven by concerns about military weakness and insufficient defense spending
choices.explanation: Military spending was a secondary concern; the primary drivers in both cases were economic grievances (trade, deindustrialization) and cultural anxiety (immigration, identity) rather than defense policy.

choices.text: Both reflected elite consensus that neoliberal globalization had failed and required alternatives
choices.explanation: Both Brexit and Trump drew primarily from working-class and non-college-educated voters, not elite consensus; many elites in both countries opposed Brexit and Trump.

choices.text: Both were caused primarily by foreign interference in domestic political processes
choices.explanation: While Russian interference was alleged in both cases, the structural explanations focus on domestic economic and cultural grievances as the primary causes — foreign interference may have amplified but did not create the conditions.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has generated resistance and reform movements

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Rwandan genocide of 1994, in which approximately 800,000 Tutsi were killed in 100 days, revealed which failure of the post-Cold War international order?

choices.text: The inability of international institutions to prevent or stop mass atrocities without political will from major powers
choices.explanation: Despite UN peacekeepers being present, the international community — led by the US and France — refused to intervene or even use the word 'genocide,' revealing that the post-Cold War 'new world order' lacked the political will to prevent mass atrocities when no strategic interests were at stake.

choices.text: The inability of international trade agreements to prevent economic conditions leading to ethnic conflict
choices.explanation: The Rwanda genocide was primarily a product of ethnic mobilization by extremist Hutu leadership and colonial-era ethnic categorization, not trade policy failures.

choices.text: The failure of Cold War deterrence structures after the Soviet collapse
choices.explanation: Cold War deterrence was specifically about superpower nuclear conflict; the Rwanda genocide was an intra-state mass killing unrelated to nuclear deterrence structures.

choices.text: The ineffectiveness of economic sanctions as a tool for preventing human rights violations
choices.explanation: Economic sanctions were not applied to Rwanda in any meaningful way during the genocide; the failure was one of military non-intervention, not inadequate economic pressure.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how globalization has affected political developments in the late 20th century

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The United Nations' 'Responsibility to Protect' (R2P) doctrine, adopted in 2005, established which principle of international relations?

choices.text: Nations have an unconditional right to intervene militarily in any state where human rights violations occur
choices.explanation: R2P does not establish unconditional intervention rights; it requires UN Security Council authorization and is intended as a last resort after diplomatic options are exhausted.

choices.text: When a state fails to protect its citizens from genocide, war crimes, or ethnic cleansing, the international community has responsibility to intervene
choices.explanation: R2P qualified absolute sovereignty by establishing that states hold sovereignty conditionally — on the duty to protect their populations; failure of this duty triggers international responsibility to protect civilians through diplomatic, legal, or military means.
